summary: Fowler shares candid reflections on LLMs and software development, arguing that current surveys fail to account for different usage workflows—particularly the gap between autocomplete and agentic editing approaches. He admits uncertainty about programming's future, criticizing anyone who claims to know what LLMs will mean for the profession. He acknowledges AI is definitively a bubble but notes that bubbles still create real value and survivors. The piece emphasizes that hallucinations are LLMs' core feature, not a bug, and warns about serious security risks from AI agents with data access.
key_points: Surveys on LLM productivity are flawed because they don't distinguish between autocomplete usage and agentic file-editing workflows
Nobody knows what LLMs mean for programming's future, and anyone claiming otherwise is 'talking from an inappropriate orifice'
AI is definitely a bubble, but bubbles generate real value and some companies survive—the dot-com bust killed pets.com but not Amazon
Hallucinations aren't a bug but THE feature of LLMs—all they do is produce hallucinations, some of which happen to be useful
LLMs introduce non-determinism into software engineering, potentially forcing the field to adopt tolerances like other engineering disciplines
AI agents with data access, untrusted content exposure, and exfiltration capabilities create a 'lethal trifecta' of security risks
money_quotes: Anyone who says they know what this future will be is talking from an inappropriate orifice.
Is AI a bubble? OF COURSE IT'S A BUBBLE. All major technological advances have come with economic bubbles.
When the dot-com bubble burst, it killed pets.com, it killed Webvan… but it did not kill Amazon.
Hallucinations aren't a bug of LLMs, they are a feature. Indeed they are the feature. All an LLM does is produce hallucinations, it's just that we find some of them useful.
We shouldn't ask an LLM to calculate an answer than we can calculate deterministically.
Software Engineering is unusual in that it works with deterministic machines. Maybe LLMs mark the point where we join our engineering peers in a world on non-determinism.
I find LLMs are quite happy to say 'all tests green', yet when I run them, there are failures. If that was a junior engineer's behavior, how long would it be before H.R. was involved?
The entire concept of an agentic browser extension is fatally flawed and cannot be built safely.
key_insight: We're still in the early experimental phase of understanding how to use LLMs effectively, and the most valuable approach is paying close attention to workflows rather than making confident predictions about an unknowable future.
